title: Floating Islands
subtitle: Poached meringue clouds on a pool of vanilla custard
excerpt: Egg whites whisked and poached in milk to form soft, wobbly mounds, then placed on top of a light vanilla custard made from the same poaching milk. A French classic that uses one set of eggs and one pan of milk to produce both components.
sourceNotes: Floating islands (oeufs à la neige, also called île flottante) appears in French cookery writing from the eighteenth century onward as a standard preparation in both domestic and restaurant contexts. The dish uses the same set of eggs for both components, the whites for the meringue and the yolks for the custard, an economical technique characteristic of classical French bourgeois cooking.

content.text: The technique here is efficient: you make the meringue first, poach it in the milk, and then use that milk to make the custard. The meringues pick up the flavour of the vanilla-infused milk during poaching, and the custard gets the same flavour from the same source. Nothing is wasted.

The meringues must be poached rather than baked, which gives them a soft, trembling texture rather than the crisp exterior of a baked meringue. The milk should be at the barest simmer, small bubbles rising gently, not a rolling boil, which would break the meringues apart.

items.name: Caster sugar
items.prepNote: 75 g for the meringue, 100 g for the custard

content.text: Heat {{whole-milk}} of whole milk with the vanilla pod and seeds in a wide saucepan over medium-low heat until just below simmering. Do not boil.

Whisk 4 egg whites in a clean bowl until they form firm peaks. Add 75 g of caster sugar one tablespoon at a time, whisking between additions, until the meringue is thick and glossy. Using two large spoons, shape spoonfuls into oval mounds and lower gently onto the surface of the barely simmering milk. Poach in batches of three for 2, 3 minutes each side, turning carefully with a slotted spoon. The meringues will puff and firm slightly. Lift out and drain on a plate. Reserve the infused milk.

Whisk 4 egg yolks with 100 g of caster sugar until pale. Remove the vanilla pod from the poaching milk and strain the milk. Gradually pour the warm milk over the yolks, whisking constantly. Return to the saucepan and cook over low heat, stirring continuously with a wooden spoon, for 8, 10 minutes until the custard coats the back of the spoon and holds a line when you run your finger across it. Strain into a wide, shallow serving dish or individual bowls. Cool, then chill for 1 hour.

Place the poached meringues on top of the chilled custard just before serving. Serve with a drizzle of dark caramel if you like, made from 75 g caster sugar melted with 2 tablespoons of water until amber, then drizzled over the islands from a spoon.

items.fix: The milk must barely simmer, just the occasional bubble rising to the surface. Reduce the heat and wait for it to settle before adding the meringues.
Keep the heat low and stir constantly, reaching the corners and bottom of the pan. If the custard starts to show lumps, immediately take it off the heat and whisk vigorously.
items.cause: Heat too high when cooking the custard, or it was left without stirring

items.fix: The custard should coat the back of a spoon and leave a clear line when you draw your finger across it. If very thin after 10 minutes, raise the heat slightly while stirring constantly.
items.cause: Not enough egg yolks, or cooked at too low a temperature
items.symptom: Custard is very thin and didn't thicken

items.fix: Poach for no more than 3 minutes per side. The meringues will shrink slightly as they cool but should hold their shape if assembled within 4 hours of poaching.
items.cause: Overpoached, or left to sit too long after poaching
